title: Will India's Tech Graduates Pass the AI Test?
content:
Amirul Islam is riding a massive wave of change currently sweeping through India’s software services industry. Freshly graduated from one of the prestigious Indian Institutes of Information Technology, Islam joined Infosys in 2022. He was originally hired as one of the tens of thousands of code jockeys the tech giant recruits each year. Today, the 26-year-old heads a specialized team that reports directly to the company’s chief technology officer. His team is tasked with designing advanced software that integrates seamlessly with AI agents, robotics, and cutting-edge vision systems.

The group’s newest creation is a “vibe coding” tool embedded directly in Infosys’ proprietary AI platform. This powerful tool allows the company’s consultants to develop software for clients in real time by simply describing in plain language what they need it to do. This rapid development process could entirely do away with the elaborate dance of slide decks and lengthy follow-up meetings that traditionally characterizes client interactions. Islam recalls a recent meeting where the Infosys crew delivered a fully usable prototype app to a retailer in under 30 minutes. The astonished client was visibly impressed by the sheer speed and capability of the new technology.

While AI will not entirely replace human engineers, Islam firmly believes it will doom coders who lack the ability to articulate new problems. Even for an accomplished innovator who has filed four AI-related patents in the past year, the pressure to adapt is palpable. The pervasive fear within the industry revolves around the constant struggle to keep up with rapidly evolving technologies. India’s $315 billion IT industry has long served as one of the primary engines of the country’s broader economy. For decades, coveted jobs at major firms like Infosys, Tata Consultancy Services, and Wipro offered millions of college-educated Indians a reliable path into the upper middle class.

Historically, this lucrative work involved building complex software systems for foreign airlines, Wall Street banks, and Silicon Valley tech giants. However, these industry stalwarts are now caught up in a deep existential crisis fueled by automation. Rapid and unprecedented advances in artificial intelligence have called into question whether traditional software outsourcing companies even have a long-term future. To survive, these IT giants are completely overhauling their recruiting strategies to prioritize cutting-edge skills over basic coding proficiency. New hires must now demonstrate advanced problem-solving abilities and a deep understanding of AI implementation to pass the test.
